summary: Joseph Helinski was my great-great-grandfather who made a bold decision in 1907 to immigrate to the United States to avoid being drafted into the Russian Army. With only his mother's encouragement, he left his family behind in Poland and took a risky journey by jumping off the ship near shore and swimming to land, bypassing Ellis Island. Despite the lack of official entry records, he eventually received a Certificate of Arrival in 1941 and became a naturalized citizen in 1942. He settled in Hurley, Wisconsin, where stories of him playing cards with Al Capone are still told. Joseph's independence and determination have been passed down through our family for generations.

The Russian Army was the force my great-great-grandfather Joseph Helinski was trying to avoid when he left Poland in 1907. Because Joseph was born in Russia, he faced the threat of being drafted, which prompted his family to send him to the United States. This fear of conscription was a major factor in his decision to immigrate and take such a dangerous route to enter America. The Russian Army's presence in that era had a profound impact on our family's history and migration story.

The United States was the destination my great-great-grandfather Joseph Helinski chose in 1907 to escape conscription into the Russian Army. He arrived in America under unusual circumstances, swimming ashore rather than entering through Ellis Island, and eventually settled in Hurley, Wisconsin. Although the government initially resisted granting him citizenship due to the lack of official entry records, he received his Certificate of Arrival in 1941 and naturalized in 1942. The United States became the new home where Joseph's legacy of independence and determination took root and continues in our family today.

Ellis Island was the usual entry point for immigrants coming to the United States in the early 1900s, but my great-great-grandfather Joseph Helinski never passed through it. Instead, in 1907, he jumped off the ship near shore and swam to land, which left no official record of his arrival. This choice complicated his path to citizenship decades later, as the government initially resisted recognizing him without Ellis Island documentation. To this day, we don't know why Joseph avoided Ellis Island, but his story reflects his stubborn independence.

The Certificate of Arrival was a crucial document my great-great-grandfather Joseph Helinski finally received on July 10, 1941, after years of lacking official proof of entry into the United States. Because Joseph bypassed Ellis Island in 1907, the government initially resisted granting him citizenship, but this certificate helped establish his legal presence. It symbolized not only his existence in America but also the traits of independence and determination that define our family. Shortly after, Joseph became a naturalized citizen in 1942, marking a major milestone in his immigrant journey.

Hurley, Wisconsin is the town where my great-great-grandfather Joseph Helinski settled after immigrating to the United States. It's a place filled with family history and stories, including tales of Joseph playing cards with Al Capone. Even today, our family holds reunions there, celebrating the independence and stubbornness that Joseph embodied. Hurley remains a symbol of our roots and the place where the Helinski legacy took hold over a century ago.

Al Capone is a figure who appears in family stories about my great-great-grandfather Joseph Helinski, who reportedly played cards with him in Hurley, Wisconsin. These tales, passed down by my grandmother, add a colorful dimension to Joseph's life in America during the early 20th century. While Joseph was focused on building a new life after immigrating in 1907, these anecdotes highlight the unique and sometimes unexpected connections he made. Al Capone's presence in Hurley is a reminder of the vibrant and sometimes notorious history of that era.

Poland was the homeland of my great-great-grandfather Joseph Helinski and the place where our family lived before 1907. Because Joseph was born in Russia but lived in Poland, his family feared he would be drafted into the Russian Army, prompting them to send him to the United States. This decision to leave Poland forever shaped our family's future and identity. Although Joseph never returned, the values and traits from our Polish roots, like independence and determination, continue to influence us today.
